name: Lithium
discovery:
1817 Johan August Arfwedson
isolated by William Thomas Brande 1821
reaction_water: floats, reacts moderately vigorously, disappears leaving a colourless alkaline solution with the evolution of hydrogen gas
reaction_air: reacts exothermically with the moisture and oxygen in the air and is stored under oil
other_reactions: compounds produce rose-red flame when burned

name: Beryllium
discovery:
Louis Nicolas Vauquelin(1797)
isolated by  Friedrich Wöhler & Antoine Bussy (1828)
reaction_water: no reaction
reaction_air: slowly tarnishes in air but burns brightly when heated to 1000oC
other_reactions: compounds do not impart a coulour to a Bunsen flame

name: Boron
discovery:
Joseph Louis Gay-Lussacand Louis Jacques Thénard 1808
isolated by Humphry Davy 1808
reaction_water: none
reaction_air: none at RT but will burn at very high temperatures
other_reactions: hardly reactive at all  but will react very slowly with very concentrated strong acids

name: Carbon
appearance:
diamond colourless solid reflects light when cut
graphite slippery grey solid
discovery:
Joseph Louis Gay-Lussacand Louis Jacques Thénard 1808
isolated by Humphry Davy 1808
boiling_point: Graphite sublimes and diamond turns to graphite when heated strongly
density:
diamond 3.5 g cm−3(at 0 °C, 101.325 Kpa)
graphite 2.6 g cm−3(at 0 °C, 101.325 Kpa)
reaction_water: none
reaction_air: none at RT but will burn when ignited at very high temperatures with the elease of a large amount of heat

name: Nitrogen
discovery: Daniel Rutherford (1772)
reaction_water: none
reaction_air: none

name: Oxygen
discovery: Daniel Rutherford (1772)
reaction_water: dissolves in water does not react with water
reaction_air: None as oxygen is part of air
other_reactions: extremely reactive supports combustion, meaning all substances require oxygen to burn

name: Fluorine
appearance: It is a pale yellow, corrosive gas,
discovery:
Joseph Black (1755)
isolated by Humphry Davy (1808)
reaction_water: Water burns in fluorine with a bright flame
reaction_air: No reaction
other_reactions: It is a pale yellow, corrosive gas, which reacts with practically  everything. Finely divided metals, glass, ceramics, carbon, and even water burn in fluorine with a bright flame.

name: Sodium
reaction_water: floats, reacts vigorously, melts into a ball, disappears leaving a colourless alkaline solution with the evolution of hydrogen gas
reaction_air: reacts exothermically with the moisture and oxygen in the air and is stored under oil
other_reactions: compounds produce yellow flame when burned

name: Magnesium
discovery:
Joseph Black (1755)
isolated by Humphry Davy (1808)
reaction_water: sinks, magnesium ribbon floats, reaction takes years but will react with steam to form magnesium oxide and evolve hydrogen
reaction_air: reaction in air is extremely slow at room temperature but burns with a very bright white light when ignited
other_reactions: compounds do not impart a colour to a flame but metal burns with an extremely bright white light

name: Aluminium
discovery:
predicted by Antoin Lavosier 1787
Isolated by Friedrich Wohler 1827 Named by humphry Davy 1807
reaction_water: no reaction
reaction_air:
corrosion resistance but 
powder burns extremely
 brightly in a flame

name: Silicon
discovery:
predicted by Antoin Lavosier 1787
Isolated by Berzelius 1823 Named by Thomas Thompson 1817
reaction_water: no reaction
reaction_air: no reaction
other_reactions: no reaction with acids but will react with strong bases

name: Phosphorous
appearance: Several different types of solid crystal, white phosphorous and red phosphorous most common
discovery:
Hennig Brand 1669
recognised as an element by Antoine lavosier
reaction_water: Can be stored in water to prevent it from reacting with oxygen in the air. Completely unreactive with water
reaction_air:
white phosphorous 
glows in the dark 
when exposed to
 oxygen with a very 
faint tinge of green 
and blue
other_reactions: highly flammable and pyrophoric (self-igniting) on contact with air and is very toxic

name: Sulfur
appearance: a yellow solid with a pungent smell
discovery:
Chinese,before 2000BC
recognised as an element by Antoine Lavosier
reaction_water: powder floats on water
reaction_air:
sulfur melts to a 
blood-red liquid
and burns with a blue flame which is best viewed
 in the dark The 
combustion product 
is a very pungent acidic gas
other_reactions:
reacts with iron once started reaction continues
 glowing red hot

name: Chlorine
appearance: It is a pale green, corrosive gas with a distinctive choking odour (We would now refer to the odour as similar to bleach)
discovery:
Carl Wilhelm Scheele (1774)
Recognized as an element by
Humphry Davy (1808)
reaction_water: dissolves in water forming an acidic solution
reaction_air: none    heavier than air
other_reactions: It is a pale green, corrosive gas, which reacts with practically  everything.

name: Potassium
discovery:
Humphry Davy (1807)
Isolated by Humphry Davy (1807)
reaction_water: floats, reacts violently, bursts into a lilac flame, disappears leaving a colourless alkaline solution with the evolution of hydrogen gas
reaction_air: reacts exothermically with the moisture and oxygen in the air and is stored under oil
other_reactions: compounds produce a lilac flame when burned

name: Calcium
appearance: silver grey sometime yellow metallic solid
discovery: Humphry Davy (1808)
reaction_water: sinks reacts vigorously leaves an alkaline solution and a white precititate with the evolution of hydrogen gas
reaction_air: reaction with oxygen or air is extremely slow at room temperature
other_reactions: compounds burn with a brick red flame

name: Rubidium
discovery: Robert Bunsen (1861)
reaction_water: floats, reacts violently sometimes explosively, bursts into a lilac flame, disappears leaving a colourless alkaline solution with the evolution of hydrogen gas
reaction_air: reacts exothermically with the moisture and oxygen in the air and is stored under oil
other_reactions: compounds produce a lilac flame when burned

name: Bromine
appearance: red-brown liquid surrounded by a red-brown vapour
discovery: Antoine Jérôme Balard and Leopold Gmelin (1825)
reaction_water: dissolves in water leaving a yellow orange solution acidic solution known as bromine water
reaction_air: none
other_reactions: reacts with metals in the presence of water

name: Strontium
appearance: silver grey sometime yellow metallic solid
discovery:
William Cruickshank (1787)
isolated by  Humphry Davy (1808)
reaction_water: sinks reacts extremely violently, leaves an alkaline solution and a white precititate with the evolution of hydrogen gas
reaction_air: reaction with oxygen or air occurs at room temperature, and therefore strontium is stored under oil
other_reactions: compounds burn with an orange red flame

name: Iodine
appearance: lustrous metallic grey-black solid , which sublimes to a purple vapour
discovery: Bernard Courtois (1811)
reaction_water: reacts to form a weakly acidic solution
reaction_air: none
other_reactions: reacts with metals in the presence of water

name: Caesium
discovery: Robert Bunsen (1861)
reaction_water: reacts explosively even with ice at temperatures, disappears leaving a colourless alkaline solution with the evolution of hydrogen gas
reaction_air: reacts exothermically with the moisture and oxygen in the air and is stored under oil

name: Barium
discovery:
Carl Wilhelm Scheele (1772)
isolated by  Humphry Davy (1808)
reaction_water: sinks reacts extremely violently, leaves an alkaline solution and a white precititate with the evolution of hydrogen gas
reaction_air: reaction with oxygen or air occurs at room temperature, and therefore barium is stored under oil
other_reactions: compounds burn with an apple green flame
